title: Mechanical Work Calculator W = F·d·cos(θ) | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Mechanical Work: W = F × d × cos(θ)
description: Calculate work done by force over distance at any angle. Use the W = F·d·cos(θ) formula instantly, free.
intro: Physics laws are universal and deterministic. Learning formulas helps you solve real-world problems involving motion, energy, forces, waves, thermodynamics, and electromagnetism. This calculator helps you determine work done by a force over a distance, accounting for the angle between force and displacement. Free, fast, and always current through 2026.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **W = F × d × cos(θ)**. Direct calculation using your input values. Determine work done by a force across a distance at any angle θ. Fast and free.
explanation:
**Applied physics** solves everyday problems—from free fall to home electricity. Formulas tested through centuries of experimentation.

## How It Works

This calculator computes work done by a force moving an object across a distance at any angle using the formula W = F · d · cos(θ). It validates typical ranges and alerts you to unusual values.

## Important Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current through 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.q: When is work equal to zero?
faq.a: Work is zero when the force is perpendicular to the direction of motion (θ = 90°). For example, carrying an object horizontally—gravity does zero work even though you're moving.
Yes. Negative work occurs when force and displacement point in opposite directions. Friction or air resistance doing work against motion are common examples.

faq.q: What are the units of work?
faq.a: The SI unit is Joules (J), where 1 J = 1 N·m. In imperial units, work is measured in foot-pounds (ft·lb). 1 J ≈ 0.738 ft·lb.

faq.q: How is work different from power?
faq.a: Work is energy transferred by force over distance. Power is the rate at which work is done: Power = Work ÷ Time, measured in Watts (W = J/s).

faq.q: How do I measure force in the real world?
faq.a: Use a dynamometer (force gauge) to measure directly, or calculate using F = m × a (mass times acceleration). Common sources: weights, springs, or Newton's laws.

faq.q: What if multiple forces act on an object?
faq.a: Calculate the work done by each force separately, then sum them: W_total = W₁ + W₂ + ... Each force contributes its own work based on its magnitude and angle.
Yes, it uses standard physics formulas validated internationally. Results are precise for the values entered. For critical applications, verify with professional calculations.

faq.q: Do you store my calculation data?
faq.a: No. All calculations run directly in your browser. We don't send your data to any server or store it anywhere.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
